mnemonics.title: Destructor Call Order
mnemonics.content: DESTRUCTOR: Derived Ends Then Base (DETB) - Derived destructor executes first, then Base. Remember: Construction builds up (Base→Derived), Destruction tears down (Derived→Base).

mnemonics.title: Virtual Destructor Purpose
mnemonics.content: VIRTUAL: V-table In Runtime Enables Late Inherited Tearing - Virtual destructor allows runtime lookup to call correct destructor chain when deleting through base pointer.

mnemonics.title: Virtual Destructor Rule
mnemonics.content: POLYMORPHIC: Polymorphic Objects Need Virtual Destructors Always - Any class used polymorphically (with base pointers/references) must have a virtual destructor.

acronyms.usage: Destructors implement RAII pattern - resources acquired in constructor, released in destructor automatically.

acronyms.expansion: Remember: Acquire In Initialize, Release In Invoking destructor
acronyms.usage: Memory/resources allocated in constructor, deallocated in destructor.
Derived Immediately, Parent Finally - Destructor call order: Derived first, then Parent (Base).

visualTips.visualization: Think of building a house: Foundation (Base) built first, then walls (Derived). Demolition: Walls torn down first, then foundation. Draw arrows showing Base→Derived for construction and Derived→Base for destruction.
Draw a v-table diagram showing how base pointer stores v-table address. When delete is called, runtime looks up destructor in v-table to call derived destructor first, ensuring proper cleanup chain.

Draw a triangle: Base at top, Derived below. Show arrow from base pointer pointing to derived object. When delete happens, show dotted line going through v-table to call derived destructor, then up to base destructor.

analogies.analogy: Destructors are like janitors who clean up after a theater show. When the show ends (object goes out of scope), janitors (destructors) come in to clean up (release resources) so the theater can be ready for the next show.
A virtual destructor is like a master key that can open any lock (call the right destructor). A non-virtual destructor is like a regular key that only opens one specific lock (base class).
Think of Russian nesting dolls. The outer doll (Derived) is opened first, then inner doll (Base). Destructors work in reverse - outer (Derived) is cleaned up first, then inner (Base).
